turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Jackson has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 23-3, Coinbase versus Suski.

turns.name: JUSTICE JACKSON
turns.text: Arbitration is a matter of contract. This Court has long recognized that parties can have multiple layers of agreements concerning arbitration and of course as a result they can also have multiple layers of disputes. At the most basic level the parties disagree about the merits of a legal issue. In arbitration cases, there is also typically a second order disagreement about whether the parties actually agree to arbitrate the merits as opposed to having a court decide them. And there can be a third order dispute about what is known as arbitrability, which is who gets to decide whether the parties previously agreed to arbitrate the merits, an arbitrator or the court. The question in this case arises from a set of circumstances in which the parties entered into two contracts that point in opposite directions regarding the answer to the arbitrability question. Petitioner Coinbase operates a cryptocurrency exchange platform and respondents are Coinbase platform users. At first petitioner and respondents entered into a user agreement, which is a standard contract that in this case contained a delegation clause expressly designating that an arbitrator is to decide all disputes, including those about arbitrability. Later these parties agreed to the official rules of a Coinbase sweepstakes, which was another contract that had a forum selection clause sending all disputes, including those related to arbitrability, to the California Courts. Because petitioner and respondents now disagree over the arbitrability of a sweepstakes related merits issue, this Court was called upon to determine who should decide whether a subsequent contract with a forum selection clause supersedes an earlier arbitration agreement that contains an arbitration delegation clause, a judge or an arbitrator. Below both the District Court and the Ninth Circuit held that a Court gets to answer this particular arbitrability question. We agree with that conclusion and now affirm. Disputes are subject to arbitration if and only if the parties actually agree to arbitrate them. And here before either the delegation provision or the forum selection clause can be enforced, a Court, not an arbitrator, needs to decide which contract controls. Again, arbitration is just a matter of contract so the first question must always be, what have the parties agreed to? And no matter the type of agreement, disputes go to arbitration only if the parties have agreed that they should go to arbitration. In this case, given these two contracts, the question of whether the parties agreed to arbitrate arbitrability can be answered only by determining which contract applies, and per usual that question must be answered by a court. Coinbase resists this bottom line conclusion pointing to the so-called severability principle to argue that the delegation clause takes precedence and that respondents need to attack it specifically. Even assuming the severability principle is implicated here, we find that it is satisfied. Where a challenge applies equally to the whole contract and to an arbitration or delegation provision, a court must resolve that challenge. For these reasons and others explained in the Court’s opinion, we affirm the judgment below. The opinion is unanimous. Justice Gorsuch has filed a concurring opinion.
